speaker: interviewer
text: The day is April 1, 1996. We left off on the lost-- last tape. You are now in New York, living with your uncle and brother. Whereabouts in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Newark.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Newark. And what did you do after you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a tailor. I was lucky to find a job within two days, because I only had $2 in my pocket and a suitcase. That's all I came with. And when I came here, my brother and my uncle-- they had been here three months. They are almost starving. So we practically lived on the $12, which I made as a tailor. My brother, when he was lucky, he made $6 as a doughnut maker, and all the doughnuts we could eat-- which we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started out as a tailor, but I didn't like it. So six months later, after I knew a little bit what's going, on and with the help of a relative, I started in a factory as a factory hand-- unskilled. And one of the boys there was a fellow worker-- was a Jewish fellow from Brooklyn. And he was talking with another one about going to night school. He said to me, what is that? And I asked a few questions. And he said, it's right up there at-- at-- Why don't you go there? I took a course there. So what happened-- it was to be what ultimately turned-- turned into New York School of Engineering. At that time, it was called New York Technical School. And it was the last day of registration. Just I coming in that way. So the registrar said, how much schooling do you have? I had only 10 years, which I know was not enough. But in Germany, the schooling system is different.

speaker: interviewee
text: The elementary school was four years, and I had six years of high school. So I had a German diploma saying six years of high school, which was very impressive. And I had a lot of courses. The registrar couldn't read it. So I had to translate it to him. And he said, I have to send it to Trenton, but it's the last day of registration. I said, I'll make a deal with you-- not in so many words-- and we accept you. And if you cannot keep up, you just drop out. I agreed, and that's the way I started.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it's on top of working 44 hours a week at that time, I went to night school four nights a week, three hours each. So only time I did anything but study, or go to school, and working, was once a month, on a Sunday afternoon, I had a friend in New York. She was a baby nurse, and she had only one Sunday off. So that's the only time I ever did anything except keeping my nose to the grindstone. Did you enter the military in the United States? Were you in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That is also a strange story. After Pearl Harbor, I wanted to volunteer. I went to the recruiting office, and they threw me out as a-- enemy alien. I was German-- of course, the German passport and everything. I couldn't volunteer. So I went back to work. Three months later, I get a notice from the draft board to register. Through a-- be drafted-- I was registered. I was drafted. I was one of the earliest one in the factory to be drafted. So they made a party for me, and everybody was thinking luckily it's not me. I went to the draft board. They looked at me, and said, something is wrong with your papers. So the next day I was back at the factory, shamefaced that I didn't make it. Three months later, I get another letter. Your papers are in order. Come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the draft board-- passed, was shipped to Fort Dix. There they looked at my papers-- enemy alien, non-combatant service. You have to go to the medics. So I was in the medics. I was in their youth-- how I fit into the medics, which I didn't-- and there was another Jewish young doctor who is interviewing. And when he asked what qualifies me for being here. I said, evidently, they don't trust me with a gun. So he looked me up and down and said, I know how you feel, soldier. I am Jewish, too. Just roll with the punches. Everything will be all right. Six weeks later I got a call to report to battalion headquarters. And the sergeant says that, son, what have you done? Here we have orders to you send under escort to a place. We cannot tell you where. Something must be wrong with you. I said, I don't know. So the next morning, at 4 o'clock, I was put on a train under escort-- not in the car-- probably said-- realized I couldn't find my way around. And early afternoon, I wound up in military intelligence-- just like that. And it was the interrogation camp. Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Camp Ritchie, in Maryland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a-- what was your rank at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still a recruit. I hadn't even finished basic training.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your basic training was in Fort Dix?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My basic training was in Camp Pickett as a medic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Virginia. Camp Pickett is-- was near Blacksburg, not too far from Belvoir, which is better known.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were sent to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp Ritchie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is in Maryland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Maryland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your training like for military intelligence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly classroom work, some fieldwork, and general training. But since the emphasis was mostly on intelligence-- interrogating, I didn't get much field training. And I wasn't a good soldier as such.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The training course lasted two months, but since there was no place to go, we stayed there until May '44. And I was doing various jobs-- retraining, sending out the maneuvers, and the usual-- hurry up and wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in-- as I said, in May '44, we were put into interrogating teams and shipped to England. When we arrived, they didn't like us. They didn't trust us. What do we do with Germans? So-- You were-- you were all--

speaker: interviewee
text: We all had German accents. We all were-- we all were-- most of us were German refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of you Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of us, too. Of course, officers were not. And then we had a little friction later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What command were you assigned to at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the 3rd Army under Patton, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your division?

speaker: interviewee
text: The division was the 35th infantry division.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was your commanding officer? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a General Baade, B-A-A-D-E. And we were at division headquarters. We were only attached at the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this? Where in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was actually in Portway, which is a small town near Devonshire. So-- we-- D plus 30-- we were prepared for D-Days. And our unit went into action D plus 30. We could get across the channel with dry feet. Even so, we were prepared to swim if necessary. And we were assigned to a regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you already given some combat training, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were assigned to a regiment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What regiment?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the 30-- 20th infantry regiment of the 35th division. And once they heard our accent, they wanted to disarm us and make us probably KPs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had orders. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is they?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was regimental troops.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted to disarm you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because they didn't trust us. Here we were assigned to regimental headquarters, and they didn't want to have any German spies amongst them. But we had orders to be used as interrogators, which we were. Somehow the refugees were so much better than the American officers who were in charge of us that-- in particular, the one in charge of our particular unit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to mention his name, for I have to say a few very nasty things. He couldn't handle it-- didn't want to handle it. And in that particular case, his German was just what he learned from his parents. And not-- no-- not much schooling in Germany-- he went to Rochester University. And I have a suspicion that his parents were members of the Bund.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he did not-- but he was a target. Whatever it is, without going into detail-- it's a story in itself. Within a week, he was relieved of duty, and I was put in charge of the unit. Your rank at this time now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My rank at that time was staff sergeant. And they tried to replace me from intelligence headquarters with another the officer. And they put-- put our colonel, who at that time liked me and what I did; insisted that I will be in charge of his interrogation no matter what they do. So I sent in-- said now-- Evidently I did a good enough job, that six weeks later, I was recommended for a commission. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKING] But I was a nasty fellow, and very cocky at that time-- got a few enemies. So it took about six months until I finally got the commission. It was just the last weeks in the war. It was-- was held up in various places out of spite. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That is my interrogation. You have-- you want to know anything about interrogation? Yes. So I'm-- wanna know who you interrogate. Were you-- now, where were you now? You're still in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, in France. OK. What were your duties?

speaker: interviewee
text: The duties-- it was a special kind of interrogation. We-- --took the prisoners right as they were captured, even before they were processed, and found-- tried to find out all the tactical information they had, which was who were they, what was in front of us, how-- just basically what is facing us, our line-- so strategical aspect they were us interrogated. They were-- that was handled in the prisoner of war camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went into particular prisoner of war camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was in the-- at the front line. I was with the troops. And even before the prisoner of war were turned over to the military police and to the-- the guards, I interrogated them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the first one they actually saw and talked to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- now, who were these? Were these-- these were German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: German soldiers-- mostly infantrymen-- who were captured at the front-- some who were actually captured-- some who liked to be captured. And I wanted to make sure-- what my-- was our job in our team-- it was a two-man team-- to find out as much as possible, as fast as possible, so that it could be used tactically against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to extract the information? What were your methods?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were usually very talkative, and basically, they actually helped us. The Germans helped us with their brutality. In one case, I only had to indicate that I knew how to treat them, because I was a former inmate of Dachau; that they did everything and anything just to please me so I didn't get angry at them. And they talk quite freely. So big question, actually, was to ask the right questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- so what were those questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that you cannot get from a book. You have to look at the guy, have to figure out what can he know? Because once a prisoner get accustomed, or get-- to say, I don't know, and get away with, he will use it more and more all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first questions I asked were strictly questions I know he could answer. And he knew I knew he has the answer. So he didn't get away with saying, I don't know. And that way, I work more and more in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, they didn't know much, because it was a-- a front of the war, with front lines, and they were stuck in the fox holes for two, three weeks, or whatever time it was-- didn't move around much. And they didn't know what's going on. Their field of-- was limited.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the only way it really would-- became meaningful, getting more and more of them, and put them to guess a lie and you saying it. That what's in-- the original one knew. It's-- you draw a picture. You make a picture from the various interrogations-- nothing of itself very important. Was there anybody infamous that you interrogated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- weren't at the front line. Those people didn't fight at the front line.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to somebody who refused-- was there anybody that refused to give information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very few. See, many started out refusing, but a few did refuse. We were limited by the Geneva Convention. So I couldn't get physical, but I could scare him. Just the fact that I was from Dachau, that I was in Dachau, scared most of them enough to-- did what I wanted. And also, the Germans were not only brutal to other people-- sad, really-- it's a old man, just as brutal. I probably was the first man who talked to some of those people in a nice way for-- for the last year. They were accustomed to being barked at. I talked to them. And they reacted. They didn't know what it is. They were accustomed to be tortured, beaten, hit, asked questions, strung up. The only things they were not prepared for is being treated nicely. And that threw them off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you able to extract a great deal of information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That's why-- how I got the-- in charge. It's-- that's how I got a commission. And I got decorated. I got the bronze star, which was a very nice dedication. Not only that-- when I was on the special assignments after the war, and our division was yanked out to be sent over to Japan, the colonel of the regiment wrote me a personal letter of thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in France when you did this interrogation?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over. It's-- we started in Normandy, went all the way through, with the 3rd Army, towards-- through central France, and wound up in-- in December near the German border into Lodz opposite [NON-ENGLISH] and Mannheim. And I remember it's-- sorry-- we got maps. When we got the maps for the next day or the-- for the next action, it were-- the target was Kaiserslautern. And I was very excited about getting on German soil. But two hours later, the Battle of the Bulge began. And we were pulled out and pushed into this battle up north near Passau. And I wound up near Passau. So that ended that one.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] How was that for you being now on the other side, so to speak? You were first a prisoner in Germany, and now you were able to be doing something else on the other side. How was that for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed every--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed every minute of it. It's, to me, the army was child's play. The rigors-- there were no rigors for me. It's easy as anything ever I had for a long time. I was fed. I was clothed. And what was the most important thing in Dachau-- we suffered individually, and we had-- we just had to take it. Here we could help each other. I had others around me who were willing to help, able to help, looked out for me, and I could look out for other people. It's a completely different complexion. It was a revelation.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was your rank now as a commissioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: I come to ascend-- a lieutenant. But the way I got that, you said where-- where through France? We went all the way through France. We were flown into Passau. After that, took the northerly route across the Rhine and [NON-ENGLISH] through the northern part of Germany-- Hildesheim and so on. And I wound up in Mecklenburg, across the Elbe. I was one of the troops across-- who got across the Elbe. Later on, that territory had to be ceded to the Russian advancing troops. And three weeks before the armistice, our division was for the first time put back in the reserve. And that's where finally, the papers for my commission caught up with me. And I was made a lieutenant three weeks before the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your serial number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two. The enlisted man serial number was 32468061, and the officer's one was 2015956.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you crossing back into Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure. It was great-- great satisfaction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other incidences happen while you were an interrogator that you care to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so much about interrogation. It's-- as I said, it was routine. I had a few run-ins with somebody, but that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Run-ins with who?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was prisoners who didn't want to talk. I convinced them. I said I was the boss-- very fast interrogation. So it's not that much to brag about that. But one thing I wanted to talk about is I mentioned that we were put-- during the armistice, we were in the reserve-- pulled back. I just had gotten my commission. And shortly-- when we were used to go to prisoner of war camps, and sort out and release them by-- released some Germans back to their hometowns-- and while I was on that duty, by divisions of the 5th infantry division was put out to be shipped to Japan.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were shipped around and reorganized, and attached to other units in Germany-- being-- having my own unit. We were three men at that time-- a four man unit with two jeeps. We were more or less independent. And I traveled around looking for relatives. And I went to several places.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally, I went to my hometown, Lehrberg. And I got there. And the thrill of my life, so to speak, was when I saw some people that I know-- friends and enemies-- and I suddenly showed up as an officer. That was an impression. [LAUGHS] And [LAUGHS] I-- oh, and they went like a live fire through the whole village. And they came running. But it had one more effect, which was much more immediate. I told a friend of mine where I was stationed at that time. And three days later, two of my cousins, who had been deported in 1942, came to Lehrberg. They were-- survived-- came there to look for what was left, and they found through my friend, my address. And the boy borrowed a bicycle. And he came to me. And so I found him.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Had they been in prison?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were both in Stutthof. They were deported with the older brother and her mother. The father had died of a war wound-, I mentioned before. And when they got there, the mother was shot immediately. The boy-- the older boy was trained as an auto mechanic, and, as such, he was in charge of the commandant's car, and-- which saved him. And he literally was able, with help of the commandant, to pull his younger brother out of the gas chamber line and make him his assistant. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Meanwhile, his sister had been pulled out of the camp and was in a factory near Gdansk-- hard labor. After the war-- during the liberation-- I don't know exactly whether it was before or after-- they had typhoid in the camp. And the older brother died of typhoid. [DRINKING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the younger boy, somehow, through Sweden-- he was all the way up there near Gdansk, in the East Prussia-- got back to Germany, made his way to Leipzig. You know the big railroad yard-- one of the biggest? And by chance, he met there his sister. What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Itzhak Selling and Katie Selling. They lived with us in the same village. It was the other Jewish couple in our village. So I was able to get him over-- I mean over to-- to the relatives. They had a sister here, and they put the apparatus in motion to get him to the United States. I couldn't do much more, except while they were there, I fed them, make sure they got better housing, they got clothing, and made it easy for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in-- in Germany after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was shipped out of Germany in December of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was shipped back to the United States on a freighter, and got there in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got there in January?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?
